title: Copper oxide wipe decoration
subtitle: Applying and wiping back copper oxide to colour raised texture
excerpt: Copper oxide is brushed over a textured bisqueware surface and then partially wiped back with a damp sponge, leaving colour in the recesses and a clean surface on the raised areas. The technique reads well on carved, impressed, or relief-textured stoneware. Requires kiln access.
sourceNotes: Copper oxide wipe-back technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Clay fired once at cone 06, producing a porous surface that absorbs oxides and glazes.
A metal-based colourant that is suspended in water and applied to ceramic surfaces before a glaze coat.
A glassy surface coating applied to bisqueware that seals the clay and produces the finished surface colour and texture after firing.
A pyrometric measure of heat-work. Cone 6 is roughly 1220 degrees C.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.

Copper carbonate and copper oxide are toxic if inhaled or ingested. Mix wet only. Wear nitrile gloves when handling oxide solutions. Ventilate the kiln firing: copper-bearing glazes release fumes at peak temperature. Never open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C.
Wipe-back
oxide
decoration amplifies surface texture. Colour catches in the carved lines and recesses. The high points stay pale after the sponge removes the surface layer. A clear or celadon
glaze
fired over the oxide seals the surface and allows the copper to show its characteristic green.
When to use it
Apply copper oxide wipe-back to
bisque
that has a defined surface texture: impressed patterns, carved lines, sprigged relief, or stamped designs. The technique does not have much impact on smooth, untextured surfaces.
What you need
Bisque-fired textured stoneware piece
Copper carbonate: 1 teaspoon mixed with 50 ml water to make an oxide wash
Soft flat brush for applying the oxide
Pottery sponge and clean water for wiping back
Clear or celadon glaze for the top coat
Step-by-step
Mix the copper carbonate with water to a thin wash consistency, roughly the colour of weak tea. Stir well before use as the oxide settles quickly.
Brush the oxide wash liberally over the entire textured surface, working it into the recesses. Apply two coats for consistent colour. The bisque absorbs the wash rapidly.

Allow to dry for 10 minutes. Wring out the pottery sponge until barely damp. Wipe the raised surfaces of the piece in light passes, removing the surface layer of oxide while leaving the oxide in the recesses. Do not press hard: the goal is to lift colour from the peaks only.
Allow the piece to dry fully. Apply a clear or celadon glaze over the oxide layer. The glaze seals the oxide and will reveal the copper colour after firing.
Fire to
cone
6. Copper in a clear glaze at cone 6 produces green. Copper in a reduced atmosphere produces red, but this tutorial covers oxidation firing only.
What it looks like when it is working

After firing, the impressed or carved lines hold a deeper green than the raised surfaces, which appear pale or the base glaze colour. The contrast is subtle in a celadon glaze and stronger in a clear. The copper colour varies from mint green to forest green depending on the concentration and the specific glaze chemistry.
Common mistakes
Oxide wash too concentrated: the copper will crawl or flake from the surface during firing. Use a thin wash, not a paste.
Wiping back too much: if the sponge is too wet or the wiping too vigorous, all the oxide is removed from the recesses as well. The sponge should be barely damp.
Glaze applied over wet oxide: wait for the oxide to dry fully before glazing or the layers will mix unpredictably.
